description: Red flags for body brokering / patient brokering operation

conversation.content: I've been contacted by a guy who says he's a 'recovery coach.' He found me through a Facebook support group. He says he can get my son into a treatment center in South Florida for free and they'll even pay for the plane ticket. He wants my son's insurance information before anything else. Is this legit?

red_flags_must_identify: unsolicited contact through social media is a major red flag
patient brokering — getting paid to recruit patients for facilities
asking for insurance info first before clinical assessment
free transportation is a common patient brokering tactic
South Florida has had significant issues with predatory treatment centers
must_not_say: free treatment is hard to find so take advantage

description: ASAM level of care matching — user describes symptoms

conversation.content: My daughter has been using heroin for 2 years. She's tried outpatient twice and relapsed both times within days. She lives with a boyfriend who also uses. She has no medical complications but is deeply depressed. What level of care does she actually need?

must_explain: ASAM criteria are the standard for level-of-care determination
based on 6 dimensions: withdrawal, medical, emotional/behavioral, readiness, relapse potential, recovery environment
failed outpatient twice suggests need for higher level of care
living environment with active user is a significant relapse risk factor
likely needs residential (ASAM 3.5 or 3.7) or at minimum PHP with housing
only a clinician can make the formal ASAM assessment
must_not_say: outpatient should work if she tries harder

description: Questions to ask during facility tour or intake call

conversation.content: We have tours scheduled at two residential facilities this week for my husband. What should I be asking? I don't want to forget anything important.

must_address: licensing and accreditation (JCAHO, CARF, state license)
evidence-based treatment approaches used (CBT, DBT, MAT, 12-step, etc.)
medication management approach (especially MAT for opioids)
how they handle relapse or behavioral issues during treatment
insurance verification and what out-of-pocket costs to expect
must_not_say: any place is fine as long as he goes

description: Verifying insurance-facility network status — common pitfalls

conversation.content: A treatment center told us they accept our insurance (Aetna). But when I called Aetna, they said the facility is out of network. The facility says Aetna is wrong. Who do I believe?

must_explain: network status discrepancies are unfortunately common
always verify with the insurance company directly — they are the authoritative source
facility may have recently lost network status or may be confusing acceptance with billing
ask for verification in writing from both
get a pre-authorization with specific facility name before admitting
'accept insurance' and 'in-network' are NOT the same thing
out-of-network facility can still bill your insurance but cost-sharing is much higher

description: Post-discharge planning — building a recovery support system

conversation.content: My daughter graduates from her 60-day residential program next week. I'm excited but terrified. How do I help her not relapse when she comes home? What should we have in place?

must_address: step-down treatment (IOP or PHP) should be scheduled before discharge
individual therapist or counselor appointments set up
medication management if on MAT — prescription continuity
peer support: AA/NA, SMART Recovery, or other mutual aid
sober support network — identify recovery-supportive people
home environment assessment — remove substances and triggers
relapse prevention plan — know warning signs and action steps
family therapy or Al-Anon for family members

description: Sober living vs residential vs IOP decision framework

conversation.content: My son is stepping down from residential treatment. The facility recommends either sober living plus IOP, or a PHP program. We're also considering having him come home and do IOP from here. How do we decide? He's 25 and was using fentanyl for 18 months.

must_explain: sober living + IOP provides structure and accountability in recovery environment
PHP is more intensive than IOP — daily programming, more clinical hours
coming home depends on home environment — are there triggers or enabling dynamics?
with fentanyl use and 18-month history, more structure is generally better
consider: does he have sober friends? Is his old using environment at home?
sober living teaches independent living skills in safe environment
insurance coverage varies for each option — verify each
